_note: SYNTHETIC DEMO FIXTURE — not investment advice, not real CAVA disclosures. Scripted diligence call for the Diligence-Call Copilot demo. The buy-side analyst (the customer) grills the sell-side researcher. Each researcher turn carries an `expected` block = the GOLDEN coverage verdict the copilot's coverage engine should produce for that turn. This file is both the stage script and the test fixture (tests/test_demo_transcript.py validates the arc; the future coverage engine is graded against it).
summary: Go in with 8 questions. Six tick green cleanly. Q2 (new-unit economics) goes amber on a vague answer -> the copilot fires a grounded follow-up -> closes green a hair below model. Q4 (traffic vs price) answers green but contradicts the note's traffic-led model -> inconsistency flag. Q7 (catering) sits grey until the copilot nudges with time running out -> closes green. Hang up all-green with an auto-updated thesis.

turns.speaker: analyst
turns.text: Thanks for making time. Let's start with the build. Your note has CAVA at about seventeen percent unit growth next year. How derisked is that, and how much of the pipeline is actually signed?

turns.speaker: researcher
turns.text: The FY26 class is effectively derisked. North of ninety percent of next year's openings are signed leases, and they've got line of sight roughly two years out as they push into inland markets. Pipeline depth isn't the constraint here, it's their own build-and-operate cadence.

extracted_facts: >90% of FY26 openings are signed leases
~2 years of pipeline visibility
expansion into inland markets; constraint is build/operate cadence, not pipeline

turns.copilot_surfaced: Q1 -> GREEN. Grounding: note-unit-pipeline ('~17% FY26E unit growth, fully covered by signed-lease pipeline').

turns.speaker: analyst
turns.text: Good. On margins, can the twenty-five percent restaurant-level margin hold with wage inflation, the steak rollout, and new-unit drag?

turns.speaker: researcher
turns.text: I think so. Steak is accretive to check even if the food cost is a touch higher, pricing has more than covered wage inflation, and the only real drag is the new-unit mix, which is already in the model. Fleet margin holds around twenty-four to twenty-five.

extracted_facts: steak accretive to average check despite higher food cost
pricing has covered wage inflation
new-unit drag already modeled
fleet restaurant-level margin holds ~24-25%

turns.copilot_surfaced: Q3 -> GREEN. Grounding: filing-fleet-metrics ('restaurant-level profit margin ~25%').

turns.speaker: analyst
turns.text: Let's go deeper on the new units themselves. Are the recent vintages opening at the AUV you model, and how are the 2023 and 2024 classes maturing?

turns.speaker: researcher
turns.text: Yeah, the new classes are opening strong, we're really encouraged. Returns are healthy, low-teens cash-on-cash early on, and the team feels great about the cohort. It's honestly one of the best new-unit stories in the whole group.

extracted_facts: claims new classes 'opening strong' (no AUV given)
cited 'low-teens cash-on-cash' early (vague, dodged the modeled AUV/margin)
expected.followup: They dodged the number. Your note models a $2.6M new-unit AUV and ~20% year-one restaurant-level margin. Ask specifically: what AUV is the 2024 class actually opening at, and what is its year-one restaurant-level margin?

turns.copilot_surfaced: Q2 -> AMBER (partial). Follow-up card fires, grounded in note-new-unit-econ. This is the hero moment.

turns.speaker: analyst
turns.text: Let me pin that down. Your note models a two-point-six million new-unit AUV and about twenty percent year-one restaurant margin. Is the 2024 class actually opening at two-point-six, and what's the year-one restaurant-level margin on that specific cohort?

turns.speaker: researcher
turns.text: Fair. The 2024 class is opening a touch below the mature fleet, call it around two-point-four million AUV, not two-point-six, and year-one restaurant-level margin is running high-teens, roughly eighteen percent, ramping to the low-twenties by year two. So a hair light versus the model, but the ramp shape is intact.

extracted_facts: 2024-class new-unit AUV ~$2.4M (vs $2.6M modeled)
year-one restaurant-level margin ~18% (vs ~20% modeled)
ramps to low-20s margin by year two; ramp shape intact
expected.thesis_delta: New-unit AUV ~$2.4M vs $2.6M modeled (~8% light); year-one margin ~18% vs ~20%. Lower the new-unit AUV assumption; ramp shape unchanged.

turns.copilot_surfaced: Q2 -> GREEN. Thesis-delta card: new-unit AUV/margin revised down modestly. (Below-model number = new info, NOT a self-contradiction with the note — the note's number was a forecast, and the analyst asked for the actual.)

turns.speaker: analyst
turns.text: That's the kind of honesty I want. On the comp, what's the traffic versus price split right now? Is traffic still positive?

turns.speaker: researcher
turns.text: Honestly, most of this year's comp has been price. They took double-digit menu price over the last few quarters, and traffic's been roughly flat, slightly positive in the best quarters, but the heavy lifting is price.

extracted_facts: current comp is price-led, not traffic-led
double-digit menu price taken over recent quarters
traffic roughly flat / slightly positive

contradiction.detail: Note (Meridian, note-comp-quality) models TRAFFIC-LED comp: ~+5% traffic, ~+3% price. Researcher now says comp is PRICE-LED: double-digit price, flat traffic. Comp quality is materially weaker than the published model — directly hits the premium-multiple argument.

expected.thesis_delta: Comp is price-led, not traffic-led as modeled. Downgrade comp-quality assumption; re-test the premium-multiple justification and the durability of the runway.

turns.copilot_surfaced: Q4 -> GREEN with red INCONSISTENCY flag citing note-comp-quality. Thesis-delta card: comp quality downgraded.

turns.speaker: analyst
turns.text: Okay, that changes how I read the comp. On frequency, where's the loyalty relaunch, and what's the digital mix doing?

turns.speaker: researcher
turns.text: Loyalty relaunch is live, it's points-based now, more flexible than the old threshold model, and the early frequency data is encouraging. Digital's holding around a third of sales, roughly thirty-five percent, which is a healthy margin mix.

extracted_facts: loyalty relaunch live, points-based / more flexible than prior threshold model
early frequency lift encouraging
digital sales mix ~35%

turns.copilot_surfaced: Q5 -> GREEN. Grounding: filing-loyalty-digital ('digital ~35%; loyalty relaunched, points-based').

turns.speaker: analyst
turns.text: And the moat. How defensible is the Mediterranean lead against Chipotle, Sweetgreen, the new entrants? How much pricing power is left?

turns.speaker: researcher
turns.text: The category lead is real, they're effectively defining Mediterranean fast-casual the way Chipotle did with burritos, and the new entrants are subscale on real estate and supply chain. On pricing, after the double-digit increases I'd say they're closer to the end of the easy pricing than the start. That's the watch item.

extracted_facts: category-defining lead (Chipotle/burrito analogy)
new entrants subscale on real estate and supply chain
pricing power closer to exhausted after recent double-digit increases ('watch item')
expected.thesis_delta: Pricing power likely near its limit — reinforces the Q4 concern that price-led comp is not repeatable.

turns.copilot_surfaced: Q6 -> GREEN. Grounding: filing-category-position. Note for analyst: this reinforces the Q4 price-led-comp flag (pricing runway is closing).

turns.speaker: analyst
turns.text: Last of my main ones, is all this growth self-funded, or do they need to raise? Any appetite for M&A or buybacks?

turns.speaker: researcher
turns.text: Self-funded. Operating cash flow covers the build, no equity raise needed in the model, and the balance sheet's clean. No real M&A or buyback appetite, they're reinvesting everything into new units, which is the right call at these returns.

extracted_facts: unit growth self-funded from operating cash flow; no raise needed
clean balance sheet
no M&A/buyback appetite; reinvesting in units

turns.copilot_surfaced: Q8 -> GREEN. At this point Q1-Q6 and Q8 are green; Q7 (catering) is still GREY. ~90 seconds left.

turns.speaker: analyst
turns.prompted_by_copilot: Copilot nudge: 'Q7 (catering / new formats) still uncovered — ~90s left.'
turns.text: Before we run out of time, one I haven't hit: catering, and any newer formats. What's the read on the catering rollout, and is there anything on smaller-format or new-daypart prototypes?

turns.speaker: researcher
turns.text: Catering's an underrated layer, it's growing nicely as an incremental occasion with attractive economics, and they're testing a smaller-format prototype and a new-daypart, breakfast-style concept. But those are early and not in anyone's numbers yet, so call it free optionality.

extracted_facts: catering growing as incremental occasion with attractive economics
smaller-format prototype in test
new-daypart/breakfast concept early; not in current numbers (optionality)

turns.copilot_surfaced: Q7 -> GREEN. ALL 8 QUESTIONS GREEN. Zero holes.

turns.speaker: analyst
turns.text: That's everything on my list. I appreciate you being straight on the new-unit AUV and the price-versus-traffic mix, that was really helpful.

thesis_delta.summary: Thesis intact but de-rated on quality. Two assumptions moved against us, both surfaced live: new-unit AUV is running ~$2.4M vs $2.6M modeled (year-one margin ~18% vs ~20%), and same-restaurant sales are price-led, not traffic-led as the note claims, with pricing power near its limit.
thesis_delta.net: Lower the new-unit AUV, downgrade comp quality, and re-test whether the premium multiple still holds. Growth/funding (Q1, Q3, Q8) and category moat (Q6) confirmed; optionality (Q7) intact.
